note: Hand-authored ground truth for the detection pipeline. Offsets are codepoint indices into sample-document.txt; for every span text == sample[start:end] (verified programmatically). `spans` are sensitive and SHOULD be detected; `allowlist` entries look like PII but must be suppressed.

spans.note: Full name, anchored by the title 'Ms.' immediately before it.
Same claimant, bare first name on second mention. Links to p1 by casefold of the shared token 'margaret'.
SAME number, international format. Same normalized_value as ph1 -> the two must be linked as duplicate occurrences.
Supervisor, introduced only relationally ('her supervisor, ...'). No title, no label, no format cue -- pure NER/semantic catch.

allowlist.why: Public underwriting company named only for reference, not the claimant's PII. Anything inside this range must not appear in the final span list.

adversarial_cases.name: name-with-no-anchor
adversarial_cases.why: 'Daniel Okafor' has no title, no preceding label, and no structural shape. Regex and dictionary passes cannot catch it; it exists to prove the NER/LLM layer earns its place. A pipeline that only does regex+dictionary WILL miss it.

adversarial_cases.name: duplicate-phone-two-formats
adversarial_cases.why: The same mobile written as '0412 887 905' and '+61 412 887 905'. A naive digits-only normaliser yields '0412887905' vs '61412887905' and FAILS to link them; correct E.164 canonicalisation (drop national trunk '0', prepend +61) yields '+61412887905' for both. Tests the merge step's duplicate linking.
